The procedure must display a message indicating the old price, new price, and any error if the book does not exist. **Model Solution:** ```sql CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE proc_discount ( p_book_id IN NUMBER, p_discount IN NUMBER ) IS v_old_price books.price%TYPE; v_new_price books.price%TYPE; v_title books.title%TYPE; BEGIN -- Fetch current price SELECT title, price INTO v_title, v_old_price FROM books WHERE book_id = p_book_id; -- Apply discount v_new_price := v_old_price - (v_old_price * p_discount / 100); -- Update the table UPDATE books SET price = v_new_price WHERE book_id = p_book_id; COMMIT; DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Book: ' || v_title); DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Old Price: ' || v_old_price); DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('New Price: ' || v_new_price); EXCEPTION WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Error: No book with ID ' || p_book_id); END proc_discount; / ``` **Invocation:** ```sql SET SERVEROUTPUT ON; BEGIN proc_discount(101, 10); -- Apply 10% discount END; / ``` **Mark Distribution:** - Correct procedure signature with IN parameters: **1 mark** - `SELECT ... INTO` logic: **2 marks** - Calculation and UPDATE: **2 marks** - Exception handling and output: **2 marks** --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Create a **PL/SQL function** `fn_total_value` that takes no parameters and returns the total inventory value (`SUM(price × stock)`) of all books. Demonstrate calling it from a SQL `SELECT` statement. **Model Solution:** ```sql CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_total_value RETURN NUMBER IS v_total NUMBER := 0; BEGIN SELECT NVL(SUM(price * stock), 0) INTO v_total FROM books; RETURN v_total; END fn_total_value; / ``` **SQL Invocation:** ```sql SELECT fn_total_value AS total_inventory_value FROM dual; ``` **Mark Distribution:** - Correct function signature with RETURN clause: **2 marks** - Aggregate `SUM` inside SELECT INTO: **3 marks** - NVL for NULL safety and demonstration of SQL call: **2 marks** --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper] **Consider the schema:** ```sql CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id NUMBER PRIMARY KEY, cust_name VARCHAR2(50), amount NUMBER(10,2), status VARCHAR2(20) DEFAULT 'PENDING' ); ``` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Write a **BEFORE INSERT row-level trigger** `trg_validate_order` that ensures the `amount` is always positive. If `amount <= 0`, the trigger must raise a user-defined error using `RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR` with error code `-20001` and a meaningful message. **Model Solution:** ```sql CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_validate_order BEFORE INSERT ON orders FOR EACH ROW BEGIN IF :NEW.amount <= 0 THEN RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR( -20001, 'Order amount must be positive. Provided: ' || :NEW.amount); END IF; -- Auto-set default status if NULL IF :NEW.status IS NULL THEN :NEW.status := 'PENDING'; END IF; END trg_validate_order; / ``` **Testing:** ```sql INSERT INTO orders (order_id, cust_name, amount) VALUES (1, 'Ravi', 5000); -- ✅ Success INSERT INTO orders (order_id, cust_name, amount) VALUES (2, 'Meena', -200); -- ❌ ORA-20001 raised ``` **Mark Distribution:** - Correct trigger syntax (BEFORE, FOR EACH ROW): **2 marks** - `RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR` with proper code: **3 marks** - `IF` condition and message construction: **2 marks** --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Create an **AFTER UPDATE trigger** `trg_audit_order` that records every status change of an order into a `order_audit` table with columns `audit_id`, `order_id`, `old_status`, `new_status`, and `change_time`. Include a `WHEN` clause to avoid logging identical-status updates. **Model Solution:** ```sql CREATE TABLE order_audit ( audit_id NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, order_id NUMBER, old_status VARCHAR2(20), new_status VARCHAR2(20), change_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT SYSTIMESTAMP ); CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_audit_order AFTER UPDATE OF status ON orders FOR EACH ROW WHEN (OLD.status != NEW.status) BEGIN INSERT INTO order_audit (order_id, old_status, new_status) VALUES (:OLD.order_id, :OLD.status, :NEW.status); END trg_audit_order; / ``` **Mark Distribution:** - Correct trigger event and timing: **2 marks** - `WHEN` clause to filter same-status updates: **2 marks** - INSERT into audit table using `:OLD` and `:NEW`: **3 marks** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These!)** > 1. **Forgetting `IS` keyword** — Many students write `CREATE PROCEDURE name(p IN NUMBER) AS` (Pascal-style). The correct Oracle PL/SQL delimiter is **`IS`** (or `AS` in some dialects, but `IS` is the KTU-preferred form). > 2. **Missing `/` terminator** — In SQL*Plus / SQL Developer, a stored object compilation requires a **forward slash `/` on its own line** after `END;`. Without it, the block is buffered and never compiled. > 3. **Confusing `:OLD` with `OLD.`** — In a trigger body, the **colon prefix** is mandatory (` :NEW.salary `). Writing `NEW.salary` (no colon) is a **compilation error** worth −1 to −2 marks. > 4. **Using `COMMIT` inside a trigger or function** — Strictly **forbidden**. Triggers inherit the parent transaction. Putting `COMMIT` inside raises `ORA-04092`. > 5. **Returning a value from a procedure with `RETURN` statement** — `RETURN` in a procedure only exits the subprogram; it does **not** pass a value. Use `OUT` parameters instead. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion. > 6. **Not enabling `SET SERVEROUTPUT ON`** — Procedures that use `DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE` will appear "empty" during evaluation. Always start the invocation block with `SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] A **procedure** is a precompiled PL/SQL block with optional `IN/OUT/IN OUT` parameters, invoked explicitly. - [x] A **function** must return exactly one value via `RETURN`, has only `IN` parameters, and can be used inside SQL expressions. - [x] A **trigger** fires automatically on DML events; it has no parameters but uses `:NEW` and `:OLD` pseudorecords. - [x] Trigger timing: **`BEFORE`** runs before the DML (used for validation/defaulting), **`AFTER`** runs post-DML (used for auditing/notification). - [x] Row-level triggers (`FOR EACH ROW`) execute once per affected row; statement-level triggers execute once per DML statement. - [x] The `WHEN` clause optimizes triggers by skipping the body when the condition is false. - [x] `CREATE OR REPLACE` allows redefinition without explicit `DROP` (a board exam time-saver). - [x] Always use `%TYPE` and `%ROWTYPE` to inherit datatypes and avoid hard-coded type mismatches. - [x] `DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE` requires `SET SERVEROUTPUT ON` to be visible in the console. - [x] `RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001 to -20999, 'message')` is the standard way to raise user-defined exceptions in triggers. - [x] The compilation errors of stored objects are queryable via `SELECT * FROM user_errors WHERE name = UPPER('object_name');` - [x] Triggers and their triggering DML share a **single transaction** — an unhandled exception in a trigger rolls back the DML that fired it. ---

topics.title: Creation of packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERL508 - PCERL508 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Creation of packages # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Package (Java / KTU Definition):** A *package* in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related **classes**, **interfaces**, **enumerations**, and **sub-packages**. It is essentially a **folder/directory structure** used to group logically related types, prevent **naming collisions**, enforce **access control**, and provide a mechanism for **modular software design** as mandated by the KTU 2024 Object-Oriented Programming Laboratory syllabus. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCERL508** lab course, "Creation of packages" is Experiment 2, which tests the student's ability to **define, compile, import, and execute** a Java program where the source files are distributed across **two or more user-defined packages**, demonstrating encapsulation and modular compilation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Office Drawer Cabinet"** > Think of your computer's hard drive as a giant office room. If you throw all your files (classes) into one pile on the floor, finding any specific file becomes impossible — this is what happens when you write Java code without packages. A **package** is like a **labeled filing cabinet drawer** inside that room. Each drawer holds a *related set of files* (e.g., the `math` drawer holds all files about mathematics). Now, when someone in another office needs a calculator file, they don't go rummaging through the whole room — they simply open the **math** drawer from the **utilities** cabinet. This is exactly how Java packages work: they group related classes, give them a unique *fully qualified name*, and allow other programs to import only what they need. ## 1.3 Why Packages? — The Three Pillars | Pillar | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | **Namespace Management** | Prevents *class name clashes* when two libraries define a class with the same simple name (e.g., `java.util.Date` vs `java.sql.Date`). | | **Access Protection** | Provides *default* and *protected* access scopes that depend on the **package boundary** for resolution. | | **Modular Reusability** | Enables *team-based development* — different teams can own different packages and release them as JAR libraries. | ## 1.4 Standard KTU 2024 Naming Convention > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Convention:** KTU evaluation scripts assume the directory name **exactly matches** the package declaration. If the package is `ktu.lab.geometry`, the folder hierarchy must be `ktu/lab/geometry/`. Violation of this causes the `NoClassDefFoundError` runtime exception and results in a **2-mark deduction** per KTU board examiner guidelines. **Two reserved package prefixes you must know:** - **`java.*`** — Core Java SE packages (built into the JDK). - **`javax.*`** — Extended Java packages (historically). All **user-defined** packages must use **lowercase** identifiers (e.g., `com.ktu.student`, `mypack`). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Package — The Two Required Artifacts To create a package in Java, exactly **two artifacts** must exist on the disk: 1. **The `package` declaration** — written as the **first non-comment statement** inside the `.java` source file. 2. **The matching directory structure** — the folder path must be **identical** to the dotted package name, with each dot (`.`) representing one folder level. ```text Source code: package ktu.lab.geometry; Folder path: ktu/lab/geometry/ (Linux/macOS) ktu\lab\geometry\ (Windows) ``` ## 2.2 The Three Logical Layers of a KTU Package Experiment ### Layer 1 — Defining a Package Use the `package` keyword at the top of every file belonging to the package. ```java package ktu.lab.geometry; public class Circle { public double area(double r) { return Math.PI * r * r; } } ``` ### Layer 2 — Compiling a Package The **`javac`** compiler must be invoked from the **root directory** (the directory that *contains* the `ktu` folder), and the **destination folder** is specified with the `-d` flag. ```text javac -d . ktu/lab/geometry/Circle.java ``` The `-d` flag tells the compiler to **automatically recreate** the folder structure and place the generated `.class` files inside it. ### Layer 3 — Importing a Package Two equivalent syntaxes are accepted by the KTU evaluator: | Syntax | Use Case | KTU Mark Implication | |---|---|---| | `import ktu.lab.geometry.Circle;` | Importing a *single* class. | **Preferred** in viva voce. | | `import ktu.lab.geometry.*;` | Importing *all* classes from the package. | Allowed but discouraged for large packages. | | Fully qualified name: `ktu.lab.geometry.Circle c = new ktu.lab.geometry.Circle();` | No `import` statement used. | Loses **1 mark** if not justified. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Syntax / Equation | Purpose / Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Package Declaration | `package ktu.lab.geometry;` | Must be the **first statement** in the file. | | 2 | Single-Class Import | `import ktu.lab.geometry.Circle;` | Recommended for clarity. | | 3 | Wildcard Import | `import ktu.lab.geometry.*;` | Imports entire package. | | 4 | Compilation Command | `javac -d . FileName.java` | Auto-creates folder hierarchy. | | 5 | Execution Command | `java ktu.lab.geometry.MainApp` | Uses **fully qualified** class name. | | 6 | Package Path Mapping | `a.b.c` $\longleftrightarrow$ `a/b/c/` | **Mandatory** equivalence. | | 7 | Default Access Modifier | *(no keyword)* | Visible **only within the same package**. | | 8 | CLASSPATH Variable | `CLASSPATH=.;C:\mylib` | Tells JVM where to find user packages. | | 9 | JAR Bundling | `jar cvf mypack.jar ktu/` | Packages a folder into a deployable archive. | | 10 | Built-in Package | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io` | **Auto-imported** by the compiler. | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > In the **industry**, every large Java application (e.g., Spring Boot, Apache Tomcat, Android SDK) is structured entirely as a hierarchy of packages. The KTU experiment simulates a *miniature* version of this: defining a `geometry` package, defining a `mainapp` package, compiling them independently, and resolving cross-package access. This is the **foundational skill** required for understanding **Maven/Gradle project layouts** in professional software development. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The following is the **complete, exhaustive walkthrough** of a canonical KTU Module-2 Experiment. Every command, every file, and every line of output is documented. **No step is skipped.** ## 3.1 Problem Statement (Typical KTU Question) > *Create a Java program that defines a class `Rectangle` inside a package `ktu.lab.shapes` with methods to compute `area()` and `perimeter()`. Define another class `ShapeDemo` inside a package `ktu.lab.mainapp` that imports the `Rectangle` class and displays the results.* ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution ### Step 1 — Project Root Setup Open a terminal and create the project root directory. ```text mkdir KtuPackageExp cd KtuPackageExp ``` **Logical Justification:** The **project root** is the common parent that *contains* the `ktu` folder. Both compilation and execution commands **must** be invoked from this root, otherwise the JVM cannot resolve the qualified class names. ### Step 2 — Create the Folder Hierarchy ```text mkdir -p ktu/lab/shapes mkdir -p ktu/lab/mainapp ``` The flag `-p` (Linux/macOS) creates intermediate parent directories. On Windows PowerShell, the equivalent is: ```text New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "ktu\lab\shapes" New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "ktu\lab\mainapp" ``` **Why two folders?** The package name `ktu.lab.shapes` is mapped to a **3-level directory** because each dot in the package name corresponds to **exactly one folder separator**. ### Step 3 — Write the First Source File: `Rectangle.java` Navigate into the first package folder and create the file. ```text cd ktu/lab/shapes ``` Create `Rectangle.java` with the following exact content: ```java package ktu.lab.shapes; public class Rectangle { private double length; private double breadth; public Rectangle(double length, double breadth) { this.length = length; this.breadth = breadth; } public double area() { return length * breadth; } public double perimeter() { return 2 * (length + breadth); } } ``` **Line-by-Line Logical Explanation:** - **Line 1:** `package ktu.lab.shapes;` — Declares that this class belongs to the package `ktu.lab.shapes`. The compiler will later expect a `ktu/lab/shapes/` directory to contain the compiled `.class` file. - **Line 2:** Empty line for readability. - **Line 3:** `public class Rectangle` — The class must be `public` so that classes in **other packages** can import and instantiate it. - **Lines 4–5:** Two `private` instance variables enforce **encapsulation** (a KTU CO1 outcome). - **Lines 7–10:** A **parameterized constructor** initializes the fields. Note that the `this` keyword disambiguates the field from the parameter. - **Lines 12–14:** The `area()` method returns the computed area using the **algebraic identity** $A = l \times b$. - **Lines 16–18:** The `perimeter()` method uses the identity $P = 2 \times (l + b)$. ### Step 4 — Write the Second Source File: `ShapeDemo.java` Navigate into the second package folder. ```text cd ../../mainapp ``` > *(Assuming you were inside `ktu/lab/shapes`; `../../` goes up two levels back to the project root, then descends into `ktu/lab/mainapp`.)* Create `ShapeDemo.java`: ```java package ktu.lab.mainapp; import ktu.lab.shapes.Rectangle; public class ShapeDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(10.0, 5.0); System.out.println("--- KTU Shape Calculator ---"); System.out.println("Length : " + 10.0); System.out.println("Breadth : " + 5.0); System.out.println("Area : " + rect.area()); System.out.println("Perimeter: " + rect.perimeter()); } } ``` **Line-by-Line Logical Explanation:** - **Line 1:** Declares membership in the `ktu.lab.mainapp` package. - **Line 3:** `import ktu.lab.shapes.Rectangle;` — This is the **cross-package import** that the KTU experiment is designed to test. Without this line, the compiler will throw `cannot find symbol` on `Rectangle`. - **Line 5:** `public class ShapeDemo` — Must be `public` and **must contain the `main` method** because this is the entry point of the program. - **Line 6:** The standard Java entry point signature `public static void main(String[] args)`. - **Line 7:** Instantiates the `Rectangle` object using the constructor defined in the other package. - **Lines 9–13:** Prints the results using string concatenation with the `+` operator. ### Step 5 — Compilation (from Project Root) Return to the project root directory. ```text cd ../.. ``` Compile **both** files using the `-d` flag with the current directory `.` as the destination. ```text javac -d . ktu/lab/shapes/Rectangle.java ktu/lab/mainapp/ShapeDemo.java ``` **What `-d .` does internally:** 1. The compiler reads the `package` declaration at the top of `Rectangle.java`. 2. It computes the destination path: current directory + `ktu/lab/shapes/`. 3. It creates the folder if it does not exist. 4. It writes the compiled bytecode as `Rectangle.class` inside that folder. 5. The same process repeats for `ShapeDemo.class`. If everything succeeds, the compiler prints **nothing** (silent success is the Java norm). ### Step 6 — Verify the Generated Structure ```text tree /F (Windows) tree (Linux/macOS) ``` Expected output: ```text KtuPackageExp/ ├── ktu/ │ └── lab/ │ ├── shapes/ │ │ ├── Rectangle.class │ │ └── Rectangle.java │ └── mainapp/ │ ├── ShapeDemo.class │ └── ShapeDemo.java ``` ### Step 7 — Execution Run the program using the **fully qualified** class name. ```text java ktu.lab.mainapp.ShapeDemo ``` **Expected Output:** ```text --- KTU Shape Calculator --- Length : 10.0 Breadth : 5.0 Area : 50.0 Perimeter: 30.0 ``` ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The Mathematical Verification The numerical result must satisfy the algebraic identities: $$ A = l \times b = 10.0 \times 5.0 = 50.0 $$ $$ P = 2 \times (l + b) = 2 \times (10.0 + 5.0) = 2 \times 15.0 = 30.0 $$ The program output of `Area = 50.0` and `Perimeter = 30.0` **mathematically confirms** that the cross-package method invocation works correctly. ## 3.4 Common Compilation Errors (KTU Examiner's Reference) | Error Message | Root Cause | Fix | |---|---|---| | `cannot find symbol: class Rectangle` | `import` statement is missing or folder path is wrong. | Verify the `import` line and the directory hierarchy. | | `package ktu.lab.shapes does not exist` | Compilation was run from the wrong directory. | `cd` to the **project root** and recompile. | | `Rectangle is not public in ktu.lab.shapes` | The class is declared without `public`. | Add the `public` modifier to the class. | | `NoClassDefFoundError` | `java` was invoked from a non-root directory. | Always `cd` to the project root before `java`. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Package Directory Mapping (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD rootA[Project Root: KtuPackageExp] packA1[ktu/] packA2[lab/] packA3[shapes/] packA4[mainapp/] fileA1["Rectangle.java
package ktu.lab.shapes"] fileA2["Rectangle.class
generated bytecode"] fileA3["ShapeDemo.java
package ktu.lab.mainapp"] fileA4["ShapeDemo.class
generated bytecode"] cmdA1["javac -d . Rectangle.java ShapeDemo.java"] cmdA2["java ktu.lab.mainapp.ShapeDemo"] rootA --> packA1 packA1 --> packA2 packA2 --> packA3 packA2 --> packA4 packA3 --> fileA1 packA3 --> fileA2 packA4 --> fileA3 packA4 --> fileA4 rootA --> cmdA1 cmdA1 -.generates.-> fileA2 cmdA1 -.generates.-> fileA4 rootA --> cmdA2 cmdA2 -.executes.-> fileA4 ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Package Class Invocation Flow ```mermaid graph LR mainApp["ShapeDemo.java
ktu.lab.mainapp"] importSt["import ktu.lab.shapes.Rectangle"] objInst["Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(10, 5)"] methodCallA["rect.area()"] methodCallB["rect.perimeter()"] rectClass["Rectangle.java
ktu.lab.shapes"] areaMeth["public double area()"] periMeth["public double perimeter()"] jvmOut["Console Output:
Area 50.0 Perimeter 30.0"] mainApp --> importSt mainApp --> objInst objInst --> methodCallA objInst --> methodCallB methodCallA -.invokes.-> rectClass methodCallB -.invokes.-> rectClass rectClass --> areaMeth rectClass --> periMeth methodCallA --> jvmOut methodCallB --> jvmOut ``` ## 4.3 Access Modifier Visibility Matrix (Within vs. Across Packages) ```mermaid graph TD cls1["Class A in Package P1"] cls2["Class B in Package P1
SAME package"] cls3["Class C in Package P2
DIFFERENT package"] cls4["Subclass D in Package P2"] pub["public
VISIBLE EVERYWHERE"] pro["protected
visible in same pkg + subclasses"] def["default
visible in same pkg only"] pri["private
visible in same class only"] cls1 --> pub cls1 --> pro cls1 --> def cls1 --> pri cls2 --> pub cls2 --> pro cls2 --> def cls3 --> pub cls4 --> pub cls4 --> pro ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Visualization Tip:** When drawing the access-modifier diagram in your lab record, always color-code: **green** for `public`, **yellow** for `protected`, **orange** for default, and **red** for `private`. This single diagram is worth **3 marks** in the viva voce. ## 4.4 Compilation & Execution Sequence ```mermaid graph TD stepA[Step 1: Create project root] stepB[Step 2: mkdir ktu/lab/shapes] stepC[Step 3: Write Rectangle.java with package declaration] stepD[Step 4: mkdir ktu/lab/mainapp] stepE[Step 5: Write ShapeDemo.java with import] stepF[Step 6: cd back to project root] stepG[Step 7: javac -d . both files] stepH[Step 8: Verify .class files generated] stepI[Step 9: java ktu.lab.mainapp.ShapeDemo] stepJ[Step 10: Observe console output] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepI stepI --> stepJ ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a package in Java. List any two advantages of using packages.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** A *package* in Java is a mechanism to group a set of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single namespace. It is implemented as a directory structure on the file system. > > **Advantage 1 (1 Mark):** **Namespace Management** — Packages prevent name conflicts. Two classes with the same simple name (e.g., `Date`) can coexist in different packages (`java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`). > > **Advantage 2 (1 Mark):** **Access Control** — Packages provide visibility boundaries. Members declared with *default* (package-private) access are visible only within the same package, enforcing encapsulation. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the significance of the `-d` flag used during the compilation of a Java package?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The **`-d` flag** in the `javac` command specifies the **destination directory** for the generated `.class` files (1 Mark). When a source file contains a `package` declaration, the compiler **automatically recreates the corresponding folder hierarchy** under the directory specified by `-d` and places the bytecode files in the correct subfolders (1 Mark). For example, `javac -d . MyFile.java` ensures the package's folder structure is generated relative to the current working directory (1 Mark). --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Create a Java program that defines a class `Employee` inside the package `ktu.lab.company` with the following members: `empId` (int), `empName` (String), `basicSalary` (double). Include a constructor to initialize the members and a method `calculateGrossSalary()` that returns `basicSalary + (0.20 * basicSalary)` as DA. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Define a class `EmployeeDemo` inside the package `ktu.lab.main` that imports the `Employee` class, creates two `Employee` objects, and displays their gross salaries. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) ```java // File: ktu/lab/company/Employee.java package ktu.lab.company; public class Employee { int empId; String empName; double basicSalary; public Employee(int empId, String empName, double basicSalary) { this.empId = empId; this.empName = empName; this.basicSalary = basicSalary; } public double calculateGrossSalary() { double da = 0.20 * basicSalary; return basicSalary + da; } } ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** - `package ktu.lab.company;` — must be the **first line** (1 Mark). - `public class Employee` — the `public` modifier is **mandatory** for cross-package access (1 Mark). - The constructor uses `this` to disambiguate the field from the parameter (1 Mark). - `calculateGrossSalary()` implements the formula $\text{Gross} = \text{Basic} + 0.20 \times \text{Basic} = 1.20 \times \text{Basic}$ (2 Marks). - The method is declared `public` so it can be called from another package (2 Marks). #### Model Solution — Part (b) ```java // File: ktu/lab/main/EmployeeDemo.java package ktu.lab.main; import ktu.lab.company.Employee; public class EmployeeDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Employee e1 = new Employee(101, "Arjun", 30000.0); Employee e2 = new Employee(102, "Meera", 45000.0); System.out.println("Employee: " + e1.empName + " | Gross: " + e1.calculateGrossSalary()); System.out.println("Employee: " + e2.empName + " | Gross: " + e2.calculateGrossSalary()); } } ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** - `import ktu.lab.company.Employee;` is the **mandatory cross-package import** (2 Marks). - The `main` method signature must exactly match: `public static void main(String[] args)` (1 Mark). - Two objects are instantiated using the parameterized constructor (1 Mark). - Both objects' gross salaries are computed and printed (1 Mark). - The execution command is `java ktu.lab.main.EmployeeDemo` from the **project root** (2 Marks). **Expected Output:** ```text Employee: Arjun | Gross: 36000.0 Employee: Meera | Gross: 54000.0 ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Define a class `Student` in the package `ktu.lab.school` with private fields `rollNo` (int), `name` (String), and `marks` (double array of size 5). Provide a method `computeAverage()` returning the average of the 5 marks. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Define a class `StudentReport` in the package `ktu.lab.report` that imports `Student`, creates one `Student` object with hard-coded data, and prints the student's name, roll number, and computed average. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) ```java // File: ktu/lab/school/Student.java package ktu.lab.school; public class Student { private int rollNo; private String name; private double[] marks; public Student(int rollNo, String name, double[] marks) { this.rollNo = rollNo; this.name = name; this.marks = marks; } public double computeAverage() { double sum = 0.0; for (int i = 0; i < marks.length; i++) { sum = sum + marks[i]; } return sum / marks.length; } public int getRollNo() { return rollNo; } public String getName() { return name; } } ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** - The package declaration `package ktu.lab.school;` is the **first line** (1 Mark). - All fields are `private` for **encapsulation** (a key KTU CO) (1 Mark). - The array `marks` is initialized through the constructor (1 Mark). - The `for` loop accumulates the sum, and the average is computed as $\text{Average} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} m_i}{5}$ (2 Marks). - Public *getter* methods allow read-only access to the private fields (2 Marks). #### Model Solution — Part (b) ```java // File: ktu/lab/report/StudentReport.java package ktu.lab.report; import ktu.lab.school.Student; public class StudentReport { public static void main(String[] args) { double[] m = {78.5, 82.0, 91.5, 69.0, 88.0}; Student s = new Student(1, "Rahul", m); System.out.println("Roll No : " + s.getRollNo()); System.out.println("Name : " + s.getName()); System.out.println("Average : " + s.computeAverage()); } } ``` **Explanation of Key Lines:** - `import ktu.lab.school.Student;` enables cross-package access (2 Marks). - The array initializer `{78.5, 82.0, 91.5, 69.0, 88.0}` provides 5 marks (1 Mark). - The `getRollNo()`, `getName()` accessors are used to print the data, demonstrating **data hiding** (2 Marks). - `computeAverage()` is invoked and its return value is printed (1 Mark). - Compilation: `javac -d . ktu/lab/school/Student.java ktu/lab/report/StudentReport.java` (1 Mark). **Expected Output:** ```text Roll No : 1 Name : Rahul Average : 81.8 ``` **Mathematical Verification:** $$ \text{Average} = \frac{78.5 + 82.0 + 91.5 + 69.0 + 88.0}{5} = \frac{409.0}{5} = 81.8 $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the `public` keyword on the class definition** (loses 2 marks). If the class is package-private, it cannot be imported from another package and the compiler throws *"X is not public in package Y"*. > 2. **Placing the `package` statement after `import` or class definition** (loses 1 mark). The `package` declaration **must** be the first non-comment statement. > 3. **Mismatched folder structure** (loses 2 marks). If the package is `ktu.lab.shapes` but the file is saved in `KtuPackageExp/shapes/`, the compiler will fail to find the file. > 4. **Invoking `java` from a non-root directory** (loses 1 mark). The `NoClassDefFoundError` is the most common runtime mistake observed in KTU labs. > 5. **Not showing the `tree`/`dir` output in the lab record** (loses 1 mark). Always include a screenshot or text dump of the final directory structure to prove correct compilation. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **package** is a **namespace + directory** combination in Java. - The **package declaration** must be the **first non-comment statement** of every file. - The **folder path** must **exactly match** the package name (dots become folder separators). - The `-d .` flag in `javac` **auto-generates** the folder hierarchy for compiled `.class` files. - To use a class from another package, you must either `import` it or use its **fully qualified name**. - The class must be declared `public` to be **accessible from outside its package**. - `java.lang` is the **only** package that is auto-imported into every Java file. - Compilation command pattern: `javac -d . path/to/File.java`. - Execution command pattern: `java fully.qualified.ClassName` (run from **project root**). - The `tree` (Linux/macOS) or `tree /F` (Windows) command is the standard way to **document** the package structure in the lab record. - **Default (package-private) access** is one of the most frequently tested viva questions — remember: visible only within the **same package**, invisible everywhere else. - KTU examiners check **two screenshots**: one of the `tree` output and one of the console output. Missing either can cost up to **2 marks**. - Always **close all files** in the editor and **recompile from scratch** if you encounter a `NoClassDefFoundError` — stale `.class` files in the wrong folder are a common cause. - For **larger projects**, packages are bundled into **JAR files** using `jar cvf mylib.jar foldername/`.

topics.title: Creation of cursors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL508 - PCERL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creation of cursors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PL/SQL (Procedural Language/Structured Query Language)** and **T-SQL (Transact-SQL)** programming for Enterprise RDBMS systems, a **Cursor** is a database object used to retrieve, manipulate, and traverse through a result set of rows one row at a time, rather than processing the entire set simultaneously. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PCERL508 syllabus (Module Experiment 2), cursors provide a mechanism to perform **row-by-row processing** on multi-row result sets returned by `SELECT` statements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCERL508 - Module 2):**
> A cursor is essentially a **pointer** to a private memory area (context area) allocated by the Oracle/SQL Server engine to hold the SQL statement and the result set returned by it. It allows the programmer to point to individual rows, fetch data column-by-column, and perform conditional row-level operations that are not possible with a single set-oriented SQL statement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Think of a cursor like a **bookmark in a long textbook**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Book Cursor**
> Imagine you are reading a thick novel containing 1000 pages. Instead of memorizing the entire book at once, you place a **bookmark** at a specific page. You read that page, take notes, then move the bookmark to the next page. A database cursor behaves in exactly the same way:
> - The **entire book** = the multi-row result set from a `SELECT` query.
> - The **bookmark** = the cursor pointer.
> - **Reading one page at a time** = processing rows one at a time.
> - **Turning the page** = the `FETCH` operation.
> - **Closing the book** = the `CLOSE` operation.

This row-by-row navigation is essential when business logic requires **iterative, conditional, or sequential processing** — for example, applying a different discount to each customer based on their purchase history, generating sequential employee IDs, or performing calculations that depend on previous row values.

### 1.3 Why Cursors Are Needed in PL/SQL

The **SQL language is set-oriented** — it operates on entire sets of rows simultaneously. However, **PL/SQL is procedural and row-oriented**. This fundamental impedance mismatch is bridged by cursors. KTU examiners frequently test this conceptual gap between set-based and row-based processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **SQL alone** → Set-at-a-time processing (e.g., `UPDATE emp SET sal = sal * 1.1 WHERE dept = 10;` updates all matching rows at once).
> - **SQL + Cursor** → Row-at-a-time processing (e.g., applying different rules to each employee based on a complex condition).
> - **Rule of Thumb (KTU Valuation Tip):** Cursors should be a **last resort** because they are slower than set-based SQL. Use them only when set-based logic is impossible or impractical.

### 1.4 Standard Cursor Metrics and Constants

The following constants and attributes are **explicitly mentioned in the KTU PCERL508 lab manual** and should be memorized:

| Attribute | Type | Possible Values | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `%FOUND` | Boolean | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | `TRUE` if the last `FETCH` returned a row |
| `%NOTFOUND` | Boolean | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | `TRUE` if the last `FETCH` did not return a row |
| `%ROWCOUNT` | Number | Integer | Number of rows fetched so far |
| `%ISOPEN` | Boolean | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | `TRUE` if the cursor is currently open |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of a Cursor (The 4 Mandatory Steps)

Every explicit cursor in PL/SQL must pass through **four distinct phases** in a strict sequence. KTU questions frequently test the ordering and purpose of each phase.

### Step 1: DECLARE (Declaration Phase)
The cursor is declared in the **DECLARE section** of the PL/SQL block. At this stage, the cursor is **not active** — it is merely a named, pre-compiled SQL statement waiting to be executed. Memory for the result set is **NOT** yet allocated.

```sql
CURSOR cursor_name [ (parameter [, parameter]...) ] 
   [ RETURN return_type ] IS select_statement;
```

**Why this step exists:** Pre-compilation allows the Oracle engine to validate the SQL syntax and bind variables **once**, rather than repeatedly during execution. This improves performance marginally but, more importantly, provides a **named, reusable query plan**.

### Step 2: OPEN (Allocation Phase)
The `OPEN` command executes the underlying `SELECT` statement, parses it, binds variables, allocates the **context area (private memory)**, and positions the cursor pointer **before the first row** of the result set. The cursor transitions from *inactive* to *active*.

**Why this step exists:** It separates the expensive parsing/binding operation from the actual data retrieval, allowing the application to control when the result set is materialized.

### Step 3: FETCH (Retrieval Phase)
The `FETCH` command retrieves the **current row** pointed to by the cursor into PL/SQL variables and advances the pointer to the next row. Each `FETCH` is a **single-row operation**.

```sql
FETCH cursor_name INTO variable_list;
```

**Why this step exists:** It enables row-level visibility for the procedural code, allowing `IF`, `LOOP`, and `WHILE` constructs to operate on individual rows.

### Step 4: CLOSE (Release Phase)
The `CLOSE` command releases the **context area** allocated during `OPEN`, making the memory available for other operations. After `CLOSE`, the cursor becomes *inactive* again. **Active cursors that are not closed cause memory leaks** — a common bug highlighted in KTU valuations.

**Why this step exists:** Explicit memory management is required in long-running database sessions to prevent the `ORA-01000: maximum open cursors exceeded` error.

## 2.2 Types of Cursors (High-Yield Classification)

KTU examinations frequently include a 3-mark question asking students to "list and explain the types of cursors." The classification is binary and exhaustive:

### A. Implicit Cursors
- **Definition:** Automatically created and managed by the Oracle engine whenever a `SELECT INTO`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` statement is executed directly in PL/SQL.
- **Reference name:** Always called `SQL` (e.g., `SQL%FOUND`, `SQL%NOTFOUND`).
- **KTU Tip:** No declaration is required; they are **invisible** to the programmer.

```sql
BEGIN
   UPDATE employee SET salary = salary + 5000 WHERE dept_id = 10;
   IF SQL%FOUND THEN
      DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Update successful for ' || SQL%ROWCOUNT || ' rows.');
   END IF;
END;
/
```

### B. Explicit Cursors
- **Definition:** Programmer-defined cursors that require manual `DECLARE`, `OPEN`, `FETCH`, and `CLOSE` operations.
- **Required when:** Set-based operations cannot express the required business logic.
- **KTU Tip:** This is the focus of **Module Experiment 2** — students must demonstrate complete creation syntax.

### C. Special Variants of Explicit Cursors (Bonus Knowledge)
| Variant | Purpose | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameterized Cursor** | Accepts input parameters to filter results dynamically | Frequently tested |
| **Cursor FOR Loop** | Implicitly handles OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE | Common simplification |
| **REF Cursor** | Cursor variable that can be passed between subprograms | Advanced, rarely tested |
| **FOR UPDATE Cursor** | Locks rows during fetch to prevent concurrent modification | Critical for transactional safety |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **all syntactical and logical elements** required for cursor creation in PCERL508 Module Experiment 2.

| Element | Syntax / Value | Purpose | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cursor Declaration** | `CURSOR c1 IS SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ...;` | Defines the query | 3 Marks |
| **Parameterized Cursor** | `CURSOR c1 (p_dept NUMBER) IS SELECT ... WHERE dept_id = p_dept;` | Dynamic filtering | 7 Marks |
| **Cursor FOR Loop** | `FOR rec IN c1 LOOP ... END LOOP;` | Auto-manages OPEN/FETCH/CLOSE | 7 Marks |
| **Cursor Attribute: %FOUND** | `c1%FOUND` → Boolean | Check if last fetch returned a row | 3 Marks |
| **Cursor Attribute: %NOTFOUND** | `c1%NOTFOUND` → Boolean | Used as loop exit condition | 7 Marks |
| **Cursor Attribute: %ROWCOUNT** | `c1%ROWCOUNT` → Integer | Count of rows fetched | 3 Marks |
| **Cursor Attribute: %ISOPEN** | `c1%ISOPEN` → Boolean | Check cursor status | 2 Marks |
| **FETCH Statement** | `FETCH c1 INTO v_empno, v_ename, v_sal;` | Retrieve one row | 7 Marks |
| **FOR UPDATE Clause** | `CURSOR c1 IS SELECT ... FOR UPDATE;` | Row-level locking | 5 Marks |
| **WHERE CURRENT OF** | `UPDATE emp SET sal = sal*1.1 WHERE CURRENT OF c1;` | Update fetched row | 5 Marks |
| **CLOSE Statement** | `CLOSE c1;` | Release context area | 3 Marks |
| **EXIT Condition Formula** | `EXIT WHEN c1%NOTFOUND;` | Terminate loop | 7 Marks |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cursors are extensively used in **enterprise applications** for:

1. **Payroll Systems:** Sequential calculation of bonuses, taxes, and deductions per employee.
2. **Banking Software:** Iterating through account transactions to compute monthly interest where the rule depends on account type.
3. **Inventory Management:** Processing stock movements where each item has a unique logic (FIFO, LIFO, weighted average).
4. **Data Migration Tools:** Transferring and transforming data row-by-row between heterogeneous database systems.
5. **Audit and Logging Systems:** Generating sequential audit IDs and timestamps per transactional row.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The "Hello World" of Cursors — Full Working Example

This is the **canonical KTU Experiment 2 program**. The following is an exhaustive, fully-commented PL/SQL block that creates a cursor to fetch all employees from the `emp` table, display their names, and apply a department-specific salary hike.

### Pre-Execution Setup (DDL — Data Definition Language)

The following table structure is assumed to be present in the schema. This is **standard KTU lab setup**:

```sql
-- Step 1: Create the Employee table
CREATE TABLE emp (
    emp_id     NUMBER(5) PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name   VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL,
    dept_id    NUMBER(3),
    salary     NUMBER(10, 2)
);

-- Step 2: Insert sample data
INSERT INTO emp VALUES (101, 'Arjun Krishnan',    10, 45000);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES (102, 'Meera Pillai',       20, 52000);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES (103, 'Rahul Menon',        10, 38000);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES (104, 'Sneha Iyer',         30, 61000);
INSERT INTO emp VALUES (105, 'Vivek Nair',         20, 47500);
COMMIT;
```

### Exhaustive PL/SQL Cursor Program

```sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    -- ============================================
    -- STEP 1: DECLARE the cursor (Declaration Phase)
    -- ============================================
    CURSOR emp_cursor IS
        SELECT emp_id, emp_name, dept_id, salary
        FROM   emp
        ORDER  BY emp_id;
    
    -- Declare variables to hold fetched values
    v_emp_id   emp.emp_id%TYPE;
    v_name     emp.emp_name%TYPE;
    v_dept     emp.dept_id%TYPE;
    v_sal      emp.salary%TYPE;
    v_new_sal  NUMBER(10, 2);

BEGIN
    -- ============================================
    -- STEP 2: OPEN the cursor (Allocation Phase)
    -- ============================================
    OPEN emp_cursor;
    
    -- ============================================
    -- STEP 3: FETCH in a loop (Retrieval Phase)
    -- ============================================
    LOOP
        FETCH emp_cursor INTO v_emp_id, v_name, v_dept, v_sal;
        
        -- Exit condition: when no more rows are found
        EXIT WHEN emp_cursor%NOTFOUND;
        
        -- Apply department-specific salary hike
        IF v_dept = 10 THEN
            v_new_sal := v_sal * 1.10;  -- 10% hike
        ELSIF v_dept = 20 THEN
            v_new_sal := v_sal * 1.15;  -- 15% hike
        ELSE
            v_new_sal := v_sal * 1.05;  -- 5% hike
        END IF;
        
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            'Emp ID: ' || v_emp_id || 
            ', Name: ' || v_name || 
            ', Dept: ' || v_dept || 
            ', Old Salary: ' || v_sal || 
            ', New Salary: ' || v_new_sal
        );
    END LOOP;
    
    -- ============================================
    -- STEP 4: CLOSE the cursor (Release Phase)
    -- ============================================
    CLOSE emp_cursor;
    
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Total rows processed: ' || emp_cursor%ROWCOUNT);
END;
/
```

### Expected Output

```
Emp ID: 101, Name: Arjun Krishnan, Dept: 10, Old Salary: 45000, New Salary: 49500
Emp ID: 102, Name: Meera Pillai,    Dept: 20, Old Salary: 52000, New Salary: 59800
Emp ID: 103, Name: Rahul Menon,     Dept: 10, Old Salary: 38000, New Salary: 41800
Emp ID: 104, Name: Sneha Iyer,      Dept: 30, Old Salary: 61000, New Salary: 64050
Emp ID: 105, Name: Vivek Nair,      Dept: 20, Old Salary: 47500, New Salary: 54625
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Cursor FOR Loop (Simplified Equivalent)

The Cursor FOR Loop is a **PL/SQL syntactic sugar** that internally performs the OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE operations. KTU questions often ask: *"Rewrite the above program using a Cursor FOR Loop."* The derivation is as follows:

### Logical Transformation

The Cursor FOR Loop **implicitly** generates the following code structure:

$$
\text{For Loop Body} \equiv \text{Open} \rightarrow \text{Loop}\{\text{Fetch}, \text{Exit When \%NOTFOUND}, \text{Process}\} \rightarrow \text{Close}
$$

### Equivalent Simplified Program

```sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    CURSOR emp_cursor IS
        SELECT emp_id, emp_name, dept_id, salary
        FROM   emp
        ORDER  BY emp_id;
BEGIN
    -- The implicit record 'rec' is automatically declared
    FOR rec IN emp_cursor LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            'Employee: ' || rec.emp_name || 
            ', Department: ' || rec.dept_id || 
            ', Salary: ' || rec.salary
        );
    END LOOP;
    -- Cursor is AUTOMATICALLY closed when the loop ends
END;
/
```

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Manual Cursor | Cursor FOR Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Lines of Code** | ~20 lines | ~8 lines |
| **Open Required?** | Yes, explicit | No, implicit |
| **Fetch Required?** | Yes, explicit | No, implicit |
| **Close Required?** | Yes, explicit | No, implicit |
| **Record Declaration** | Manual `v_emp_id, v_name...` | Implicit `%ROWTYPE` record |
| **Error-Prone?** | Higher (forgetting CLOSE) | Lower |
| **Use Case** | When fine-grained control is needed | For straightforward iteration |
| **KTU Exam Preference** | Tested for understanding | Tested for conciseness |

## 3.3 Parameterized Cursor Derivation

A **parameterized cursor** accepts input values at OPEN time, making the cursor reusable across multiple parameter sets. The logical derivation follows the function-call pattern:

$$
\text{CURSOR } c(p) \text{ IS } Q(p)
$$

where $Q(p)$ is a parameterized query and $p$ is the input parameter. At OPEN time, the parameter is **bound** and the query executes:

$$
\text{OPEN } c(p=10) \implies Q \text{ executes with } p=10
$$

### Complete Parameterized Cursor Example

```sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    -- Parameterized cursor declaration
    CURSOR dept_cursor (p_dept_id NUMBER) IS
        SELECT emp_name, salary
        FROM   emp
        WHERE  dept_id = p_dept_id;
BEGIN
    -- Open with parameter value 10
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('--- Department 10 Employees ---');
    FOR rec IN dept_cursor(10) LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.emp_name || ' - ' || rec.salary);
    END LOOP;
    
    -- Reopen with parameter value 20
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('--- Department 20 Employees ---');
    FOR rec IN dept_cursor(20) LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.emp_name || ' - ' || rec.salary);
    END LOOP;
END;
/
```

## 3.4 Cursor with FOR UPDATE Clause (Advanced Locking)

The `FOR UPDATE` clause **locks the fetched rows**, preventing other sessions from modifying them until a `COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK` is issued. The `WHERE CURRENT OF` clause then enables the cursor to update the **current row** directly:

```sql
DECLARE
    CURSOR sal_cursor IS
        SELECT emp_id, salary
        FROM   emp
        WHERE  dept_id = 10
        FOR UPDATE;  -- Locks all rows in dept 10
BEGIN
    FOR rec IN sal_cursor LOOP
        UPDATE emp
        SET    salary = salary * 1.10
        WHERE  CURRENT OF sal_cursor;  -- Updates the locked row
    END LOOP;
    COMMIT;
END;
/
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `FOR UPDATE` is Critical in KTU Valuation:**
> Without `FOR UPDATE`, the `UPDATE ... WHERE CURRENT OF` statement raises `ORA-01410: invalid SQL statement`. This is a **frequently-tested pitfall** worth 2-3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cursor Lifecycle State Diagram

The following Mermaid state diagram illustrates the **four mandatory phases** of an explicit cursor and the state transitions that occur at each step. This diagram is a high-yield visual aid for KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "DECLARED\nCursor defined in DECLARE section\nMemory NOT allocated" as declared
    state "OPENED\nContext area allocated\nPointer before first row" as opened
    state "FETCHING\nRow retrieved into variables\nPointer advances" as fetching
    state "CLOSED\nMemory released\nCursor inactive" as closed

    [*] --> declared : DECLARE cursor_name IS SELECT...
    declared --> opened : OPEN cursor_name
    opened --> fetching : FETCH cursor_name INTO variables
    fetching --> fetching : More rows exist\n(LOOP continues)
    fetching --> closed : %NOTFOUND becomes TRUE\n(EXIT WHEN condition met)
    closed --> [*] : Block execution complete
    opened --> closed : Explicit CLOSE cursor_name
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cursor Program

The following diagram maps the **internal architecture** of a PL/SQL program that uses a cursor, showing how the PL/SQL engine, SQL engine, and memory structures interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLSQL Block Begins] --> B[DECLARE Section\nVariable and Cursor Definitions]
    B --> C[BEGIN Section Starts]
    C --> D[OPEN cursor_name]
    D --> E[Context Area Allocated in PGA\nPrivate Memory]
    E --> F[SQL Engine Executes SELECT\nResult Set Materialized]
    F --> G[Cursor Pointer Positioned\nBefore First Row]
    G --> H[FETCH Statement Issued]
    H --> I{Row Available?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Copy Row Data into PL/SQL Variables]
    J --> K[Advance Pointer to Next Row]
    K --> L[Process Row with Procedural Logic\nIF, ELSIF, etc.]
    L --> H
    I -- No --> M[cursor_name%NOTFOUND returns TRUE]
    M --> N[EXIT WHEN condition triggers]
    N --> O[CLOSE cursor_name]
    O --> P[Context Area Released to PGA]
    P --> Q[END Block Terminates]
    Q --> R[PLSQL Block Ends]
```

## 4.3 Implicit vs Explicit Cursor — Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Need to Process\nQuery Results in PL/SQL] --> Q1{Does the SQL\nreturn multiple rows?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the logic\ncomplex with conditions?}
    Q2 -- No --> Implicit[Use IMPLICIT Cursor\nSELECT INTO statement]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Explicit[Use EXPLICIT Cursor\nwith manual control]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Do you need row-level\nbusiness logic?}
    Q3 -- No --> SetBased[Use SET-BASED SQL\nNo cursor needed]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Do you need to pass\nparameters dynamically?}
    Q4 -- No --> SimpleExplicit[Use SIMPLE EXPLICIT Cursor]
    Q4 -- Yes --> ParamExplicit[Use PARAMETERIZED EXPLICIT Cursor]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Need row-level\nlocking?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> ForUpdate[Use FOR UPDATE Cursor]
    Q5 -- No --> ParamExplicit
```

## 4.4 Cursor FOR Loop — Internal Execution Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PLSQL as PL/SQL Engine
    participant Cursor as Cursor Object
    participant Memory as Context Area
    participant Variables as PL/SQL Variables

    Note over PLSQL: FOR rec IN cursor_name LOOP
    PLSQL->>Cursor: Implicit OPEN
    Cursor->>Memory: Allocate context area
    Memory-->>Cursor: Result set ready
    Note over Cursor: Position before first row

    loop While rows exist
        PLSQL->>Cursor: Implicit FETCH
        Cursor->>Memory: Read current row
        Memory-->>Variables: Copy to implicit record
        Variables-->>PLSQL: rec.emp_name, rec.salary...
        Note over PLSQL: Execute loop body
    end

    Note over Cursor: %NOTFOUND becomes TRUE
    PLSQL->>Cursor: Implicit CLOSE
    Cursor->>Memory: Release context area
    Note over PLSQL: END LOOP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a cursor in PL/SQL. List and briefly explain the four phases of an explicit cursor lifecycle.**

**Model Answer:**

A **cursor** is a pointer to a private memory area (context area) in the Oracle server that holds the result set of a SQL query, allowing PL/SQL to process rows one at a time.

The **four phases** of an explicit cursor lifecycle are:

1. **DECLARE** — The cursor is defined in the DECLARE section with a name and an associated `SELECT` statement. No memory is allocated at this stage.
2. **OPEN** — The `OPEN` command executes the `SELECT` statement, allocates the context area in the PGA, and positions the pointer before the first row.
3. **FETCH** — The `FETCH` command retrieves the current row into PL/SQL variables and advances the pointer. This is repeated until no more rows exist.
4. **CLOSE** — The `CLOSE` command releases the context area, freeing the memory allocated during OPEN.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award full 3 marks only if all four phases are named correctly with their corresponding SQL commands (`DECLARE`, `OPEN`, `FETCH`, `CLOSE`).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between implicit and explicit cursors in PL/SQL.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Implicit Cursor | Explicit Cursor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Automatically created by the Oracle engine for all DML and `SELECT INTO` statements | Manually created by the programmer using the `CURSOR ... IS` syntax |
| **Declaration** | Not required | Required in the DECLARE section |
| **Reference Name** | Always `SQL` (e.g., `SQL%FOUND`) | Programmer-chosen name (e.g., `emp_cur%FOUND`) |
| **Open/Fetch/Close** | Automatically managed | Programmer must explicitly call `OPEN`, `FETCH`, `CLOSE` |
| **Row Count** | Always returns one or zero rows for `SELECT INTO` | Can return multiple rows processed in a loop |
| **Use Case** | Simple single-row DML operations | Complex row-by-row procedural logic |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Consider the following `employee` table:**

| Column Name | Data Type | Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| `emp_id` | NUMBER(5) | PRIMARY KEY |
| `emp_name` | VARCHAR2(50) | NOT NULL |
| `dept_id` | NUMBER(3) | |
| `salary` | NUMBER(10, 2) | |

**Write a PL/SQL block using an explicit cursor to:**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Display the name, department, and salary of all employees belonging to department 10. Apply a 12% salary increment to each of them and display the updated salary.
**(b)** [7 Marks] Modify the above program to use a **parameterized cursor** that accepts the department ID as input, and demonstrate its execution for departments 10 and 20 separately. Use the `Cursor FOR Loop` syntax.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

```sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    -- Step 1: DECLARE the explicit cursor
    CURSOR dept10_cursor IS
        SELECT emp_name, dept_id, salary
        FROM   employee
        WHERE  dept_id = 10;
    
    -- Variables to hold fetched values
    v_name     employee.emp_name%TYPE;
    v_dept     employee.dept_id%TYPE;
    v_sal      employee.salary%TYPE;
    v_new_sal  NUMBER(10, 2);
    v_count    NUMBER := 0;

BEGIN
    -- Step 2: OPEN the cursor
    OPEN dept10_cursor;
    
    -- Step 3: FETCH in a loop
    LOOP
        FETCH dept10_cursor INTO v_name, v_dept, v_sal;
        EXIT WHEN dept10_cursor%NOTFOUND;
        
        v_new_sal := v_sal * 1.12;  -- 12% increment
        
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            'Name: ' || v_name || 
            ', Dept: ' || v_dept || 
            ', Old Salary: ' || v_sal || 
            ', New Salary: ' || v_new_sal
        );
        
        v_count := v_count + 1;
    END LOOP;
    
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Total employees processed: ' || v_count);
    
    -- Step 4: CLOSE the cursor
    CLOSE dept10_cursor;
END;
/
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**
- The cursor is declared to fetch only employees from department 10 using a `WHERE` clause filter.
- The `OPEN` command materializes the result set in the context area.
- Inside the `LOOP`, each `FETCH` retrieves one row's data into the three declared variables.
- The `EXIT WHEN dept10_cursor%NOTFOUND` condition terminates the loop when all rows are processed.
- The salary increment is computed using the formula: $v_{new\_sal} = v_{sal} \times 1.12$.
- Finally, `CLOSE` releases the context area.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

```sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    -- Parameterized cursor
    CURSOR dept_cursor (p_dept_id NUMBER) IS
        SELECT emp_name, salary
        FROM   employee
        WHERE  dept_id = p_dept_id;
    
    v_total NUMBER := 0;

BEGIN
    -- Department 10 processing
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('=== Department 10 ===');
    FOR rec IN dept_cursor(10) LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            'Name: ' || rec.emp_name || 
            ', Salary: ' || rec.salary
        );
        v_total := v_total + 1;
    END LOOP;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Count: ' || v_total);
    
    -- Reuse cursor for Department 20
    v_total := 0;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('=== Department 20 ===');
    FOR rec IN dept_cursor(20) LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            'Name: ' || rec.emp_name || 
            ', Salary: ' || rec.salary
        );
        v_total := v_total + 1;
    END LOOP;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Count: ' || v_total);
END;
/
```

**Key Concepts Tested:**
- **Parameterized cursor syntax** with `p_dept_id NUMBER` as the formal parameter.
- **Cursor FOR Loop** which implicitly handles `OPEN`, `FETCH`, and `CLOSE`.
- **Cursor reusability** — the same cursor object is opened twice with different parameter values.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Write a PL/SQL program to demonstrate the use of a cursor with the `FOR UPDATE` clause.**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the significance of `FOR UPDATE` and `WHERE CURRENT OF` clauses with a suitable example on a `library` table containing `book_id`, `title`, `copies_available`, and `category` columns.
**(b)** [7 Marks] Write a complete PL/SQL block to increase the number of copies by 5 for books in the `'Science'` category where `copies_available < 5`. Display the book title and the updated copies count.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The `FOR UPDATE` clause acquires **exclusive row-level locks** on the rows returned by the cursor, preventing other transactions from modifying or locking these rows until the current transaction commits or rolls back. This is essential for **read-modify-write** workflows where data consistency is critical.

The `WHERE CURRENT OF cursor_name` clause in a subsequent `UPDATE` or `DELETE` statement refers to the **most recently fetched row** by the cursor, allowing precise targeting without specifying the row's primary key again.

**Significance Summary:**
- **Data Consistency:** Prevents the "lost update" anomaly in concurrent environments.
- **Performance:** More efficient than fetching the `ROWID` and using it in the `WHERE` clause.
- **Correctness:** Ensures the row being updated is the same row that was fetched and validated.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

```sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    CURSOR sci_cursor IS
        SELECT book_id, title, copies_available
        FROM   library
        WHERE  category = 'Science'
        AND    copies_available < 5
        FOR UPDATE;  -- Locks the selected rows
    
    v_count NUMBER := 0;

BEGIN
    FOR rec IN sci_cursor LOOP
        -- Update using WHERE CURRENT OF
        UPDATE library
        SET    copies_available = copies_available + 5
        WHERE  CURRENT OF sci_cursor;
        
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            'Updated Book: ' || rec.title || 
            ' | New Copies: ' || (rec.copies_available + 5)
        );
        
        v_count := v_count + 1;
    END LOOP;
    
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Total books updated: ' || v_count);
    COMMIT;  -- Release the locks
END;
/
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for PCERL508 Module 2**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `CLOSE` statement:** This is the **#1 cause of mark deduction** in cursor programs. KTU examiners specifically check for the `CLOSE` statement at the end. If absent, deduct **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong exit condition:** Beginners often write `EXIT WHEN emp_cursor%FOUND` instead of `%NOTFOUND`. The `%FOUND` attribute is `FALSE` when no row is fetched, so the loop never exits, causing an infinite loop. **Deduct 2 marks** for this logical error.
> 3. **Variable count mismatch in FETCH:** The number of variables in the `FETCH ... INTO` clause must **exactly match** the number of columns in the cursor's `SELECT` list. A mismatch raises `ORA-06504: PL/SQL: Return types of Result Set variables or query do not match`. **Deduct 1-2 marks** if mismatched.
> 4. **Confusing `SQL%ROWCOUNT` with `c1%ROWCOUNT`:** `SQL%ROWCOUNT` refers to the implicit cursor of the **last DML statement**, not the explicit cursor. Use the explicit cursor name's attribute.
> 5. **Omitting `COMMIT` after `FOR UPDATE`:** Without an explicit `COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK`, the locks remain until the session ends, potentially causing **deadlocks**. Examiners may deduct 1 mark for not showing `COMMIT`.
> 6. **Declaring variables with wrong data types:** Use the `%TYPE` attribute (e.g., `v_name emp.emp_name%TYPE`) instead of hardcoding `VARCHAR2(50)`. This is a **best practice** tested for 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Creation of Cursors (PCERL508 Module Experiment 2)**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ A **cursor** is a pointer to a private memory area (context area) in the PGA holding a result set.
- ✅ Cursors bridge the gap between **set-oriented SQL** and **procedural PL/SQL**.
- ✅ Explicit cursors require **DECLARE → OPEN → FETCH → CLOSE** in that exact order.

### Syntax Essentials
- ✅ Declaration: `CURSOR name [(param TYPE, ...)] IS SELECT ...;`
- ✅ Opening: `OPEN name [(param_value, ...)];`
- ✅ Fetching: `FETCH name INTO var1, var2, ...;` or `FOR rec IN name LOOP ... END LOOP;`
- ✅ Closing: `CLOSE name;`
- ✅ Exit condition: `EXIT WHEN name%NOTFOUND;`

### Cursor Attributes
- ✅ `%FOUND` — TRUE if last FETCH returned a row.
- ✅ `%NOTFOUND` — TRUE if last FETCH did not return a row (used for loop termination).
- ✅ `%ROWCOUNT` — Number of rows fetched so far.
- ✅ `%ISOPEN` — TRUE if cursor is currently open.

### Variants to Remember
- ✅ **Implicit Cursor** (`SQL` cursor) — for single-row DML operations.
- ✅ **Parameterized Cursor** — for reusable, dynamic queries.
- ✅ **Cursor FOR Loop** — for concise, auto-managed iteration.
- ✅ **FOR UPDATE** + **WHERE CURRENT OF** — for safe, concurrent row-level updates.

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- ❌ Never forget the `CLOSE` statement.
- ❌ Never use `%FOUND` as the loop exit condition.
- ❌ Always match variable count to column count in `FETCH ... INTO`.
- ❌ Always use `%TYPE` for variable declarations.
- ❌ Always include `COMMIT` after `FOR UPDATE` operations.

### One-Line Exam Answer (If Asked for Definition)
> *"A cursor in PL/SQL is a named, private SQL memory area that allows row-by-row processing of a result set, requiring the programmer to explicitly DECLARE, OPEN, FETCH, and CLOSE it."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of NoSQL databases and CRUD operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL508 - PCERL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of NoSQL databases and CRUD operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a NoSQL Database?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) Database** is a class of non-relational data storage systems designed to handle unstructured, semi-structured, and structured data using flexible schemas. Unlike traditional **RDBMS (Relational Database Management Systems)** that rely on rigid tabular schemas and SQL (Structured Query Language), NoSQL databases employ dynamic data models such as **document**, **key-value**, **column-family**, and **graph** stores. They are engineered for **horizontal scalability**, **high throughput**, and **distributed computing** environments.

### Intuitive Analogy: The Filing Cabinet vs. The Backpack

| **RDBMS (SQL)** | **NoSQL** |
|:---|:---|
| Imagine a **rigid filing cabinet** where every drawer has a pre-printed label, every folder must follow the same column structure (Name, Age, Salary, etc.), and you must place files in exact slots. | Imagine a **versatile backpack** where you can stuff books, photos, receipts, and notes — all in different shapes and sizes — without worrying about predefined slots. |
| Adding a new attribute requires altering the entire cabinet structure (schema migration). | Adding a new attribute means just throwing another item into the bag — instant and flexible. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** NoSQL databases are categorized as **BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency)** systems, contrasting with the **ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)** properties of RDBMS. This is a frequently tested distinction in University Examinations.

## 1.2 What are CRUD Operations?

**CRUD** is the acronym for the four fundamental persistent storage operations:

- **C — Create:** Insert new data records into the database.
- **R — Read:** Query and retrieve existing records.
- **U — Update:** Modify existing data records.
- **D — Delete:** Remove records from the database.

### Intuitive Analogy: A Library Card System

Think of a NoSQL collection as a **library's digital catalog**:

1. **Create** → A new book arrives; the librarian adds a new entry.
2. **Read** → A patron searches for a book; the librarian pulls the card.
3. **Update** → The book's edition changes; the librarian updates the entry.
4. **Delete** → A book is discarded; the librarian removes the card.

## 1.3 The CAP Theorem (Foundational Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):** In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee only **two out of three** properties: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**. Since network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems (P is mandatory), NoSQL systems typically choose between **AP** (e.g., Cassandra, CouchDB) or **CP** (e.g., MongoDB, HBase).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| **Term** | **Definition** |
|:---|:---|
| **Document** | A self-contained record in JSON, BSON, or XML format |
| **Collection** | A grouping of related documents (analogous to a table) |
| **Sharding** | Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple nodes |
| **Replication** | Copying data across multiple servers for fault tolerance |
| **Schema-less** | No predefined structure required for records |
| **Index** | A data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval |
| **Aggregation Pipeline** | A framework for data transformation in stages |

## 1.5 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for PCERL508

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 alignment**, this experiment maps to **CO2 (Apply NoSQL concepts to perform data operations)** and is tested at **Bloom's Taxonomy Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyze)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Taxonomy of NoSQL Databases

NoSQL databases are broadly classified into **four architectural categories**. Each serves a distinct data-modeling purpose.

### 2.1.1 Document-Oriented Databases

- **Data Model:** Stores semi-structured data as documents (typically **JSON** or **BSON**).
- **Examples:** **MongoDB**, **CouchDB**, **RavenDB**.
- **Why Use It:** Schema flexibility, native JSON support, ideal for content management and IoT.
- **How It Works:** Each document is a key-value pair where the value is a JSON object containing nested fields and arrays.

### 2.1.2 Key-Value Stores

- **Data Model:** A simple hash table mapping unique keys to values.
- **Examples:** **Redis**, **Amazon DynamoDB**, **Riak**.
- **Why Use It:** Ultra-low latency, ideal for caching, session management, and leaderboards.
- **How It Works:** Operations are limited to `GET(key)`, `PUT(key, value)`, and `DELETE(key)`.

### 2.1.3 Column-Family (Wide-Column) Stores

- **Data Model:** Data organized into rows and dynamic columns grouped into column families.
- **Examples:** **Apache Cassandra**, **HBase**, **Google Bigtable**.
- **Why Use It:** Excellent for time-series data, write-heavy workloads, and analytical queries.
- **How It Works:** Columns can vary between rows; data is partitioned by row key across distributed nodes.

### 2.1.4 Graph Databases

- **Data Model:** Nodes (entities), Edges (relationships), and Properties (attributes).
- **Examples:** **Neo4j**, **Amazon Neptune**, **ArangoDB**.
- **Why Use It:** Optimized for traversing complex relationships (e.g., social networks, fraud detection).
- **How It Works:** Uses graph traversal algorithms (BFS, DFS) over adjacency lists.

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix: SQL vs. NoSQL

| **Criterion** | **RDBMS (SQL)** | **NoSQL** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Data Model** | Tabular (rows and columns) | Document, Key-Value, Column, Graph |
| **Schema** | Rigid, predefined (DDL enforced) | Dynamic, schema-less |
| **Query Language** | SQL (standardized) | Varies (MongoDB Query Language, CQL, Cypher) |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| **ACID Compliance** | Full ACID | BASE (eventual consistency) |
| **Joins** | Fully supported | Limited or not supported |
| **Use Case** | Transactional systems (banking) | Big data, real-time web, IoT |
| **Examples** | MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle | MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Neo4j |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the most-tested concepts for KTU University Examinations under the 2024 Scheme.

| **Concept** | **Formula/Rule/Property** | **Engineering Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **CAP Theorem** | $C + A + P = 2$ (can choose at most two) | Distributed system design trade-offs |
| **Replication Factor** | $R = N_{copies} / N_{partitions}$ | Data redundancy calculation |
| **Sharding (Horizontal Partitioning)** | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ where $S_i$ is shard size | Big data distribution |
| **Eventual Consistency Time** | $T_{converge} \approx \Delta t_{replication} + \Delta t_{propagation}$ | Distributed cache synchronization |
| **MongoDB BSON Document Size Limit** | $16 \text{ MB}$ per document | Single document storage planning |
| **MongoDB Nested Document Depth** | Maximum **100 levels** of nesting | Schema design constraint |
| **Key-Value Lookup Complexity** | $O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst-case (collision) | Real-time caching |
| **Graph Traversal (BFS)** | $O(V + E)$ where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges | Social network analytics |
| **MongoDB Write Concern** | $\{w: \text{majority}, j: \text{true}\}$ | Durability tuning |
| **Index Selectivity** | $S = \frac{\text{Unique Values}}{\text{Total Records}}$ | Query optimization |

## 2.4 The CRUD Operations — Detailed Anatomy

### 2.4.1 CREATE Operation

- **Purpose:** Insert new documents/records into a collection.
- **API Equivalent:** `INSERT` (SQL) → `insertOne()`, `insertMany()` (MongoDB).
- **Atomicity:** A single document insertion is atomic; multi-document operations require explicit transactions.

### 2.4.2 READ Operation

- **Purpose:** Query documents based on filter criteria.
- **API Equivalent:** `SELECT` (SQL) → `find()`, `findOne()` (MongoDB).
- **Operators:** `$eq`, `$gt`, `$lt`, `$in`, `$regex`, `$exists` for complex queries.

### 2.4.3 UPDATE Operation

- **Purpose:** Modify existing documents based on a filter.
- **API Equivalent:** `UPDATE` (SQL) → `updateOne()`, `updateMany()`, `findOneAndUpdate()` (MongoDB).
- **Operators:** `$set`, `$unset`, `$inc`, `$push`, `$pull`, `$rename`.

### 2.4.4 DELETE Operation

- **Purpose:** Remove documents from a collection.
- **API Equivalent:** `DELETE` (SQL) → `deleteOne()`, `deleteMany()` (MongoDB).
- **Cascade Behavior:** NoSQL does not enforce referential integrity; cascade deletion must be handled in application logic.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance:** NoSQL databases power platforms like **Facebook (Cassandra for inbox search)**, **Netflix (Cassandra for user activity)**, **LinkedIn (Voldemort for key-value storage)**, **Google (Bigtable for search indexing)**, and **Uber (Schemaless on top of MySQL + Schemaless)**. For KTU students, familiarity with at least **one NoSQL system (MongoDB is recommended)** is a mandatory industry skill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Setting Up the Experiment Environment (MongoDB Example)

> [!NOTE]
> The following steps constitute the **lab procedure** for the KTU PCERL508 Module Experiment 2. **MongoDB** is the de facto standard NoSQL database taught in Indian engineering curricula.

### Step 1 — Installation Verification

Verify MongoDB installation by executing the following command in the terminal:

```bash
mongod --version
```

**Expected Output (Illustrative):**

```
db version v7.0.4
Build Info: { "version": "7.0.4", "gitVersion": "...", "modules": ["enterprise"] }
```

### Step 2 — Database and Collection Creation

**Connect to MongoDB Shell:**

```bash
mongosh
```

**Switch to a working database** (creates it if it does not exist):

```javascript
use ktu_student_db
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In MongoDB, the `use` command does not immediately create the database on disk. The database is physically created only when the first document is inserted (lazy creation).

### Step 3 — CREATE Operation (Insertion)

**Insert a Single Document:**

```javascript
db.students.insertOne({
   roll_no: "TVE21CS001",
   name: "Anand Krishnan",
   semester: 6,
   branch: "CSE",
   skills: ["Python", "MongoDB", "React"],
   cgpa: 8.7
})
```

**Insert Multiple Documents:**

```javascript
db.students.insertMany([
   { roll_no: "TVE21CS002", name: "Bhavya Menon", semester: 6, branch: "CSE", cgpa: 9.1 },
   { roll_no: "TVE21CS003", name: "Cijo Joseph", semester: 6, branch: "CSE", cgpa: 7.8 }
])
```

**Result Acknowledgment:**

```json
{
  "acknowledged": true,
  "insertedIds": {
    "0": ObjectId("..."),
    "1": ObjectId("..."),
    "2": ObjectId("...")
  }
}
```

### Step 4 — READ Operation (Retrieval)

**Read All Documents:**

```javascript
db.students.find()
```

**Read with Filter Criteria:**

```javascript
db.students.find({ semester: 6, cgpa: { $gt: 8.0 } })
```

**Logical Breakdown:**

| **Component** | **Interpretation** |
|:---|:---|
| `db.students` | Target collection within the current database |
| `find()` | Cursor-returning query method |
| `{ semester: 6 }` | Equality filter |
| `{ $gt: 8.0 }` | Greater-than operator on the `cgpa` field |

**Projection (selecting specific fields):**

```javascript
db.students.find(
   { branch: "CSE" },
   { name: 1, cgpa: 1, _id: 0 }
)
```

### Step 5 — UPDATE Operation (Modification)

**Update a Single Document:**

```javascript
db.students.updateOne(
   { roll_no: "TVE21CS001" },
   { $set: { cgpa: 8.9 }, $push: { skills: "Docker" } }
)
```

**Update Multiple Documents:**

```javascript
db.students.updateMany(
   { branch: "CSE" },
   { $inc: { semester: 1 } }
)
```

**Mathematical Interpretation of `$inc`:**

$$
\text{new\_value} = \text{old\_value} + \text{increment}
$$

For example, if a student's `semester` was 6, after `updateMany` it becomes:

$$
\text{semester}_{new} = 6 + 1 = 7
$$

### Step 6 — DELETE Operation (Removal)

**Delete a Single Document:**

```javascript
db.students.deleteOne({ roll_no: "TVE21CS003" })
```

**Delete Multiple Documents:**

```javascript
db.students.deleteMany({ cgpa: { $lt: 7.5 } })
```

**Delete All Documents (Collection Truncate):**

```javascript
db.students.deleteMany({})
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: CRUD Operator Reference

| **CRUD Phase** | **MongoDB Method** | **SQL Equivalent** | **Complexity** | **Use Case** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **C**reate | `insertOne(doc)` | `INSERT INTO ... VALUES` | $O(1)$ amortized | Adding a single user |
| **C**reate | `insertMany([docs])` | Bulk `INSERT` | $O(n)$ batched | Bulk migration |
| **R**ead | `find(filter)` | `SELECT * WHERE` | $O(n)$ unindexed | List all records |
| **R**ead | `findOne(filter)` | `SELECT ... LIMIT 1` | $O(1)$ with index | Fetch by primary key |
| **U**pdate | `updateOne(filter, update)` | `UPDATE ... WHERE` | $O(1)$ with index | Increment counter |
| **U**pdate | `replaceOne(filter, newDoc)` | `REPLACE INTO` | $O(1)$ with index | Full document swap |
| **D**elete | `deleteOne(filter)` | `DELETE ... LIMIT 1` | $O(1)$ with index | Soft delete |
| **D**elete | `deleteMany(filter)` | `DELETE FROM ... WHERE` | $O(n)$ | Bulk purge |

## 3.3 Derived Example: Aggregation Pipeline

For KTU advanced questions, the **aggregation pipeline** chains multiple CRUD-like stages:

```javascript
db.students.aggregate([
   { $match: { branch: "CSE" } },
   { $group: { _id: "$semester", avg_cgpa: { $avg: "$cgpa" }, count: { $sum: 1 } } },
   { $sort: { avg_cgpa: -1 } }
])
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Match)} &: \text{Filter docs where } branch = \text{"CSE"} \\
\text{Stage 2 (Group)} &: \text{Group by } semester, \text{ compute } \mu_{cgpa} = \frac{\sum cgpa_i}{n_i} \\
\text{Stage 3 (Sort)} &: \text{Order by } \mu_{cgpa} \text{ in descending order}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `$avg` operator computes the **arithmetic mean** of the grouped field, formally defined as:
> $$\mu = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
